# C++ bindings with pybind11

## Intro to pybind11 {.smaller}

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%" .fragment}

![](images/pybind11-logo.png){width=100%}

Can be found on [github](https://github.com/pybind/pybind11)

- Started by Wenzel Jakob at EPFL in Realistic Graphics Lab
- Widely used: PyTorch, Jax, SciPy...
- Sister [nanobind](https://github.com/wjakob/nanobind) library is also available
  - Sacrifices some C++ support for performance
  - Similar to pybind11 in API and logic
:::


:::{.column width="50%" .fragment}
- Header-only library (don't need to install it, just include)
- Well integrated into CMake build system
  - Can use [FetchContent](https://cmake.org/cmake/help/latest/module/FetchContent.html) to download it at build time
- Somewhat manual
  - You define what and how to bind yourself
  - No automatic generation of bindings
  - Gives better control over the interface (I argue it is better)
:::

::::


## High-level architecture overview {.smaller}

pybind11 uses C++ metaprogramming to allow describing bindings in a way
reminiscent of Python syntax.

:::{.incremental}
 - When possible makes use of python keywords: `class`, `def`
 - Bindings are defined incrementally:
    - First we create a module
    - Then we add functions and classes to it
 - Hides all the gritty `Py***` functions and details (you bind pure C++
   directly).
:::

::: {.callout-tip .fragment}
# C++ raw string literals

Writing well formatted Python docstrings in C++ can be a bit of a pain.
[Raw string literal helps](https://godbolt.org/z/q6T7eGExE) a lot.

It allows to write multi-line strings that will render 'as written':
```cpp
  const char * docstring = R"pbdoc(
First line.

Detailed description
    )pbdoc";
```
:::

<!-- ## C++ Method chaining pattern {.smaller}
pybind11 makes heavy use of C++ 'method chaining'
```cpp
#include <iostream>

class Adder{
    int sum;
public:
    Adder(int initial_value = 0 ) : sum{initial_value} {}
    Adder& add(int a) {
        this->sum += a;
        return *this;
    }
    int result() const {return sum; }
};

int main( ) {
    std::cout << "Sum: " << Adder(4).add(2).add(6).result() << "\n";
    return 0;
};
```
```
Output
  Sum: 12
```
Methods of a class return a reference to the class itself, so we can call another method on it and assemble them into long chains.

 - Used to create more expressive interfaces -->


## Building a module {.smaller}

```cpp

int square(int x) { return x * x; }

PYBIND11_MODULE(module_name, m) {
  m.doc() = R"pbdoc(
      Docstring goes here!
    )pbdoc";

  // We can add attributes and functions with a natural syntax
  m.attr("__version__") = "0.1.0";

  // We take and address of function when we bind them
  m.def("square", &square, "Function docstring");

  // Not necessary but we can delegate to functions to bind classes/sets of functions
  // Keeps things clean
  bind_particular_class(m);
}
```
- pybind11 gives us a macro that builds up the `PyInit_module_name` function.
- We can add attributes and functions to the module with a natural syntax.
  - `doc` method to access docstring
  - `attr` method to access attributes
  - `def` method to define functions

:::{.notes}
 - Understood how do they implement this pretty C++ wizardry.

:::


## pybind11 header system {.smaller}

```cpp
// Header with the main functionality
#include <pybind11/pybind11.h>

// We enable 'extra' features by including additional headers
#include <pybind11/stl.h>
#include <pybind11/numpy.h>
```

pybind11 has a modular header system. To enable extra features you need
to include extra headers:

- Reduces compilation time by using only necessary code
- Generally gives sensible error messages, i.e.: "Have you forgotten to include ...?"


<!-- ## Building a class {.smaller}

```{.cpp code-line-numbers="1-5|6-11|12-13"}
using py::literals::operator""_a;
...
void bind_particular_class(py::module_& m) {
  py::class_<ClassName>(m, "ClassName")
      .def(py::init<Arg1Type, Arg2Type>(), "Constructor docstring", "arg1"_a, "arg2"_a)
      .def("method_name", &ClassName::method_name, "Method docstring")
      .def_readwrite("py_attribute_name", &ClassName::cpp_attribute_name, "Attribute docstring")
      .def_property("with_getter_and_setter",
          &ClassName::getter_method,
          &ClassName::setter_method, // Will be read-only if given `nullptr`
          "Property docstring").
      // In place of a function address you can provide a lambda!
      .def_static("default", [](){ return ClassName(3,5); }, "Static method docstring");
}
```
- Uses 'method chaining' to build Domain Specific Language
  - If you squint your eyes enough, it looks like Python! ಡ_ಡ
- We need to take an address of methods, functions and members (i.e. `&` operator)
 -->

## Automatic conversions {.smaller}

pybind11 aims to be as seamless as possible:

- It will silently convert types between C++ and Python
  - Most of the time it will not matter...
  - ... but sometimes it will.
  - Copies can be heavy for large objects (e.g. long lists of strings)

::: {.fragment}
**There be dragons: narrowing conversions**


Consider
```cpp
float addone(float x) { return x + 1.0f; }
...
m.def("addone", &addone, "Add one to a float");

```

In python:
```python
>>> plunge_pyb.addone(1.0e+40)
inf
>>> 1.0e+40 + 1.0
1.0e+40
```
:::

## Automatic conversions {.smaller}

**There be dragons: mutable references**

```cpp
void addone(float& x) { x += 1.0f; }
...
m.def("addone", &addone, "Add one to a float");
```

In Python:
```python
>>> a = 1.0
>>> plunge_pyb.addone(a)
>>> a
1.0
```
Not quite the result we expected...


:::{.fragment}
**Solution**: use C++ lambdas to make wrapper

```cpp
def(m, "addone", [](float x) {addone(x); return x;}, "Add one to a float");
```
:::

## Exercise 2: Task 1 {.smaller}

You will find a familiar `splash` and `raise_exception` functions inside
the `exercise_2/src/plunge_pyb.cpp` file!

The extension module name is `plunge_pyb`, to compile and run:
```bash
pip install . -v
python -c "import plunge_pyb; plunge_pyb.splash("Joe")"
```

:::{.callout-note}
# Task

Once again create a new function `multiple_splash` that takes two arguments `name` and `count` and prints '`name` made a splash!' `count` times.

If the count is negative, it should raise a `ValueError` exception.
:::

:::{.callout-tip}
# Exception translation

pybind11 converts thrown C++ exceptions to different Python exceptions.
Consult the [translation table](https://pybind11.readthedocs.io/en/stable/advanced/exceptions.html#built-in-c-to-python-exception-translation) to know what to `raise` on C++ side.
:::

:::{.callout-caution appearance="minimal"}
Please ignore 'Task 2' parts for now ツ
:::

## STL containers {.smaller}
We bind in C++ with:
```cpp
#include <pybind11/stl.h> // <- Enables automatic conversions
....
m.def("my_len", [](std::vector<int>& vec) {
    return vec.size();
});
```
In Python we can use like:
```python
assert my_len([1, 2, 3]) == 3
```

:::{.callout-warning .fragment}
# Spot the issue with this example?
:::

:::{.callout .fragment}
We could have written `std::vector<int>`! We take a reference of a temporary copy.
:::

::: {.fragment}
This problem is deadly for scientific workflows (╯°□°)╯︵ ┻━┻
:::

## Array compatibility

Python and pybind11 have solutions to this problem ┬─┬ノ( º _ ºノ)

- The [buffer protocol](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/buffer.html) (Python standard)
  - Will not be covered here.
- pybind11 has a built-in `py::array` type that is compatible with `numpy.ndarray`
  - We will use it in the exercises
- pybind11 has similar support for the `Eigen` C++ library

## pybind11 `py:array_t` {.smaller}

Subset of functionality needed for the exercises:
```cpp
#include <pybind11/numpy.h> // <- Enables Numpy interoperability
...

py::array_t<double> my_array = py::array_t<double>(10); // 1D array of 10 doubles

my_array.mutable_at(0) = 3.14; // Mutable access to an element
auto b = my_array.at(0);       // Immutable access to an element

...
// You can accept it as an argument to a function
void my_function(py::array_t<double> arr) {
  grid.size() // You can query the size
  ...
}
```
:::{.callout-note}
This should be all you need for the exercise later on.
:::


## Callbacks

pybind11 supports `std::function` and accepts Python functions as arguments!

```cpp
#include <pybind11/functional.h> // <- Enables std::function interoperability
...

m.def("my_callback", [](std::function<void(int)> callback) {
    callback(42);
});
```
```python
>>>def func(x):
...    print(f"With: {x}")
>>> plunge_pyb.my_callback(func)
With: 42
```

##  Exercise 2: Task 2 {.smaller}

Once again we have two functions written for reference:
`print_numpy_array` and `apply_on_range` that demonstrate the use of `py::array_t`
and callback via `std::function`.

Compile and run:
```bash
pip install . -v
python -c "import plunge_pyb; plunge_pyb.print_numpy_array([1,2,3])"
python -c 'import plunge_pyb; print(plunge_pyb.apply_on_range(lambda x: x**2, 0, 10))'
```

:::{.callout-note}
# Task

Implement a new function `interpolate` that takes a `py::array_t<double>` and a `std::function<double(double)>` as arguments and returns a new `py::array_t<double>` with the function applied to each element of the input array.

Return either `std::vector<double>` or `py::array_t<double>` from the function. Chose your favourite (or do both!).

Example signature:
```cpp
py::array_t<double> interpolate_np(std::function<double(double)> func, py::array_t<double> grid) {
  ...
}
```
:::


## pybind11 summary {.smaller}

**Not covered**:

 - Binding classes
 - Parallelism (multi-threading, MPI)
 - More on array interoperability (buffer protocol, Eigen)

:::{.callout-note .fragment}
# Takeaway message
Binding C++ to Python is possible and ultimately not that hard.
:::
